main_paper.raw: {"methods":["External insight-driven correction method for uncertainty estimation in LLMs","Integration of uncertainty scores from a lightweight auxiliary model (Corrector) trained on global information with existing uncertainty estimation methods (e.g., Semantic Entropy, Shifting Attention to Relevance)"],"problems":["Inaccurate uncertainty estimation in LLMs due to inherent biases such as over-confidence and under-confidence","Inversion of uncertainty score rankings between questions the model can and cannot answer correctly","Lack of robust and generalizable uncertainty estimation methods for LLMs"],"datasets":["TriviaQA","Curated supervised datasets aligned with target LLM performance in specific domains"],"metrics":["Relative uncertainty score ranking","Absolute uncertainty estimation performance"],"results":[{"metric":"Relative uncertainty score ranking improvement over baselines","value":"Consistently surpasses all baselines in both Representative Baselines Set (RBS) and Challenging Baselines Set (CBS)"},{"metric":"Robustness and generalizability","value":"Demonstrated across diverse data domains and target models"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes an external insight-driven approach for uncertainty estimation that is orthogonal and complementary to existing methods","Introduces the use of a lightweight auxiliary model (Corrector) trained on global information to refine uncertainty scores","Demonstrates correction of inversion in uncertainty score rankings caused by LLM biases","Shows consistent and significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in both relative and absolute uncertainty estimation metrics"]}

methods: External insight-driven correction method for uncertainty estimation in LLMs

Integration of uncertainty scores from a lightweight auxiliary model (Corrector) trained on global information with existing uncertainty estimation methods (e.g., Semantic Entropy, Shifting Attention to Relevance)
problems: Inaccurate uncertainty estimation in LLMs due to inherent biases such as over-confidence and under-confidence
Inversion of uncertainty score rankings between questions the model can and cannot answer correctly
Lack of robust and generalizable uncertainty estimation methods for LLMs
datasets: Curated supervised datasets aligned with target LLM performance in specific domains

results.metric: Relative uncertainty score ranking improvement over baselines
results.value: Consistently surpasses all baselines in both Representative Baselines Set (RBS) and Challenging Baselines Set (CBS)
Demonstrated across diverse data domains and target models

novelty_claims: Proposes an external insight-driven approach for uncertainty estimation that is orthogonal and complementary to existing methods
Introduces the use of a lightweight auxiliary model (Corrector) trained on global information to refine uncertainty scores
Demonstrates correction of inversion in uncertainty score rankings caused by LLM biases
Shows consistent and significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in both relative and absolute uncertainty estimation metrics

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Semantic Entropy Probes (SEPs): linear probes trained on the hidden states of LLMs to predict semantic entropy, enabling hallucination detection from a single model generation without sampling multiple outputs","Ablation studies on SEP performance across models, tasks, layers, and token positions"],"problems":["Reliable and cost-efficient detection of hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs)","Reducing the computational cost of semantic entropy-based hallucination detection methods","Improving generalization of hallucination detection methods to out-of-distribution data"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Hallucination detection accuracy","Generalization to out-of-distribution data","Computational cost (inference cost)"],"results":[{"metric":"Hallucination detection accuracy (Llama-2-7B, SEP vs. accuracy probes)","value":"SEPs outperform accuracy probes for hallucination detection"},{"metric":"Computational cost (SEP vs. sampling-based SE)","value":"SEP achieves similar performance at nearly zero additional cost compared to 5-10x cost for sampling-based SE"},{"metric":"Generalization to new tasks","value":"SEPs generalize better to new tasks than accuracy probes, setting a new state-of-the-art for cost-efficient hallucination detection"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Semantic Entropy Probes (SEPs), which approximate semantic entropy from the hidden states of a single LLM generation, eliminating the need for multiple samples at test time","SEPs are trained to predict semantic entropy rather than accuracy, allowing for unsupervised training without ground truth labels","SEPs combine the cost-efficiency of probing methods with the semantic uncertainty quantification of sampling-based methods","Demonstration that model hidden states encode semantic uncertainty, and that SEPs can extract this information effectively for hallucination detection","SEPs generalize better to out-of-distribution data and new tasks than previous accuracy-based probes, establishing a new state-of-the-art for cost-efficient hallucination detection"]}

methods: Semantic Entropy Probes (SEPs): linear probes trained on the hidden states of LLMs to predict semantic entropy, enabling hallucination detection from a single model generation without sampling multiple outputs
Ablation studies on SEP performance across models, tasks, layers, and token positions
problems: Reliable and cost-efficient detection of hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs)
Reducing the computational cost of semantic entropy-based hallucination detection methods
Improving generalization of hallucination detection methods to out-of-distribution data

results.metric: Hallucination detection accuracy (Llama-2-7B, SEP vs. accuracy probes)
results.value: SEPs outperform accuracy probes for hallucination detection
SEP achieves similar performance at nearly zero additional cost compared to 5-10x cost for sampling-based SE
SEPs generalize better to new tasks than accuracy probes, setting a new state-of-the-art for cost-efficient hallucination detection

novelty_claims: Introduction of Semantic Entropy Probes (SEPs), which approximate semantic entropy from the hidden states of a single LLM generation, eliminating the need for multiple samples at test time
SEPs are trained to predict semantic entropy rather than accuracy, allowing for unsupervised training without ground truth labels
SEPs combine the cost-efficiency of probing methods with the semantic uncertainty quantification of sampling-based methods
Demonstration that model hidden states encode semantic uncertainty, and that SEPs can extract this information effectively for hallucination detection
SEPs generalize better to out-of-distribution data and new tasks than previous accuracy-based probes, establishing a new state-of-the-art for cost-efficient hallucination detection

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Analysis of LLM internal states (neurons, activation layers, tokens) to assess hallucination risk","Development and use of a probing estimator on LLM internal states to estimate hallucination risk before response generation"],"problems":["Hallucination in Large Language Models (LLMs) leading to unreliable and untrustworthy outputs","Lack of self-awareness or uncertainty estimation in LLMs, causing overconfident and potentially incorrect responses"],"datasets":["Over 700 datasets spanning 15 diverse Natural Language Generation (NLG) tasks"],"metrics":["Hallucination estimation accuracy","Training data exposure estimation accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Hallucination estimation accuracy (across 15 NLG tasks)","value":"84.32%"},{"metric":"Training data exposure estimation accuracy","value":"80.28%"}],"novelty_claims":["First comprehensive analysis of LLM internal states for estimating hallucination risk before response generation across a wide range of NLG tasks and datasets","Demonstration that LLM internal states can indicate both prior exposure to queries and likelihood of hallucination, enabling proactive uncertainty estimation","Use of a probing estimator to achieve high accuracy in hallucination risk estimation at run time"]}

methods: Analysis of LLM internal states (neurons, activation layers, tokens) to assess hallucination risk
Development and use of a probing estimator on LLM internal states to estimate hallucination risk before response generation
problems: Hallucination in Large Language Models (LLMs) leading to unreliable and untrustworthy outputs
Lack of self-awareness or uncertainty estimation in LLMs, causing overconfident and potentially incorrect responses
datasets: Over 700 datasets spanning 15 diverse Natural Language Generation (NLG) tasks
novelty_claims: First comprehensive analysis of LLM internal states for estimating hallucination risk before response generation across a wide range of NLG tasks and datasets
Demonstration that LLM internal states can indicate both prior exposure to queries and likelihood of hallucination, enabling proactive uncertainty estimation
Use of a probing estimator to achieve high accuracy in hallucination risk estimation at run time

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Learnable Response Scoring (LARS): a trainable, supervised scoring function for aggregating token probabilities for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs"],"problems":["Limitations of existing probability-based uncertainty estimation (UE) scoring functions for generative LLMs, such as inability to handle biased probabilities and complex semantic dependencies between tokens","Need for more reliable and calibrated uncertainty estimation in LLM-generated sequences, especially for hallucination detection"],"datasets":["Three question-answering (QA) datasets (not named in the excerpt)","A mathematical reasoning task dataset (not named in the excerpt)","Datasets in four different languages (not named in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)","PRR (Precision-Recall Rate)"],"results":[{"metric":"AUROC","value":"up to 16% improvement over SOTA scoring functions"},{"metric":"PRR","value":"up to 45% improvement over SOTA scoring functions"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LARS, a novel, learnable scoring function for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs, trained on supervised data rather than relying on heuristics","LARS captures complex dependencies between tokens and probabilities, addressing limitations of previous heuristic-based scoring functions","LARS is validated across multiple datasets and languages, showing significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods"]}

methods: Learnable Response Scoring (LARS): a trainable, supervised scoring function for aggregating token probabilities for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs
problems: Limitations of existing probability-based uncertainty estimation (UE) scoring functions for generative LLMs, such as inability to handle biased probabilities and complex semantic dependencies between tokens
Need for more reliable and calibrated uncertainty estimation in LLM-generated sequences, especially for hallucination detection

metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

results.value: up to 16% improvement over SOTA scoring functions
up to 45% improvement over SOTA scoring functions

novelty_claims: Proposes LARS, a novel, learnable scoring function for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs, trained on supervised data rather than relying on heuristics
LARS captures complex dependencies between tokens and probabilities, addressing limitations of previous heuristic-based scoring functions
LARS is validated across multiple datasets and languages, showing significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Meaning-Aware Response Scoring (MARS): a novel scoring function for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs that assigns semantic importance weights to each token in the generated sequence","A BERT-like model to efficiently assign meaning-aware importance weights to tokens in a single model pass within MARS calculation","Integration of MARS into existing probability-based uncertainty estimation (UE) methods"],"problems":["Estimating the correctness of generative LLM outputs (uncertainty estimation)","Limitations of length-normalized scoring for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs, which treats all tokens equally and ignores semantic importance","Improving reliability and accuracy of uncertainty estimation in high-stakes environments"],"datasets":["Three closed-book question-answering datasets (not named in the excerpt)","A Medical QA dataset"],"metrics":["Uncertainty Estimation (UE) performance metrics (specific metrics not named in the excerpt, but likely include AUROC, AUPR, or similar)"],"results":[{"metric":"UE performance improvement (universal and significant)","value":"MARS universally and significantly improves UE performance across all tested LLMs and datasets"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Meaning-Aware Response Scoring (MARS), a novel scoring function for generative LLMs that considers the semantic contribution of each token in the generated sequence for uncertainty estimation","Introduces a BERT-like model to efficiently assign meaning-aware importance weights to tokens in a single model pass","Demonstrates that integrating MARS into UE methods results in universal and significant improvement in UE performance across multiple LLMs and datasets","Provides a grounded framework for MARS and other scoring functions by explaining previous works' design choices from the classification perspective"]}

methods: Meaning-Aware Response Scoring (MARS): a novel scoring function for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs that assigns semantic importance weights to each token in the generated sequence
A BERT-like model to efficiently assign meaning-aware importance weights to tokens in a single model pass within MARS calculation
Integration of MARS into existing probability-based uncertainty estimation (UE) methods
problems: Estimating the correctness of generative LLM outputs (uncertainty estimation)
Limitations of length-normalized scoring for uncertainty estimation in generative LLMs, which treats all tokens equally and ignores semantic importance
Improving reliability and accuracy of uncertainty estimation in high-stakes environments
datasets: Three closed-book question-answering datasets (not named in the excerpt)
metrics: Uncertainty Estimation (UE) performance metrics (specific metrics not named in the excerpt, but likely include AUROC, AUPR, or similar)

results.value: MARS universally and significantly improves UE performance across all tested LLMs and datasets

novelty_claims: Proposes Meaning-Aware Response Scoring (MARS), a novel scoring function for generative LLMs that considers the semantic contribution of each token in the generated sequence for uncertainty estimation
Introduces a BERT-like model to efficiently assign meaning-aware importance weights to tokens in a single model pass
Demonstrates that integrating MARS into UE methods results in universal and significant improvement in UE performance across multiple LLMs and datasets
Provides a grounded framework for MARS and other scoring functions by explaining previous works' design choices from the classification perspective

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Kernel Language Entropy (KLE): a kernel-based method for uncertainty quantification in LLMs that uses positive semidefinite unit trace kernels to encode semantic similarities of outputs and quantifies uncertainty using von Neumann entropy","KLE-c: a version of KLE that operates on semantic clusters","Use of graph kernels and weight functions as concrete design choices for KLE"],"problems":["Uncertainty quantification in Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically for natural language generation","Detecting hallucinations (factually incorrect or unfaithful responses) in LLM outputs","Capturing semantic uncertainty (uncertainty over meaning) rather than lexical or syntactic uncertainty","Limitations of existing methods (e.g., semantic entropy) that only consider equivalence relations and not fine-grained semantic similarity"],"datasets":["Multiple natural language generation datasets (exact names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Uncertainty quantification performance (e.g., ability to distinguish hallucinations or model errors)","von Neumann entropy (as a measure within the method)"],"results":[{"metric":"Uncertainty quantification performance (across 60 scenarios)","value":"KLE achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) results compared to baseline methods"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Kernel Language Entropy (KLE), a novel method for uncertainty quantification in LLMs that incorporates semantic similarity via kernels and von Neumann entropy","KLE generalizes the previous state-of-the-art method (semantic entropy) and is more expressive, able to distinguish uncertainty in cases where semantic entropy cannot","KLE works for both white-box and black-box LLMs and does not rely on token likelihoods","Provides concrete, effective design choices for practical implementation (e.g., graph kernels, weight functions)","Empirically demonstrates improved uncertainty quantification performance across multiple datasets and LLM architectures"]}

methods: Kernel Language Entropy (KLE): a kernel-based method for uncertainty quantification in LLMs that uses positive semidefinite unit trace kernels to encode semantic similarities of outputs and quantifies uncertainty using von Neumann entropy
KLE-c: a version of KLE that operates on semantic clusters
Use of graph kernels and weight functions as concrete design choices for KLE
problems: Uncertainty quantification in Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically for natural language generation
Detecting hallucinations (factually incorrect or unfaithful responses) in LLM outputs
Capturing semantic uncertainty (uncertainty over meaning) rather than lexical or syntactic uncertainty
Limitations of existing methods (e.g., semantic entropy) that only consider equivalence relations and not fine-grained semantic similarity
datasets: Multiple natural language generation datasets (exact names not specified in the provided content)
metrics: Uncertainty quantification performance (e.g., ability to distinguish hallucinations or model errors)
von Neumann entropy (as a measure within the method)

results.value: KLE achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) results compared to baseline methods

novelty_claims: Proposes Kernel Language Entropy (KLE), a novel method for uncertainty quantification in LLMs that incorporates semantic similarity via kernels and von Neumann entropy
KLE generalizes the previous state-of-the-art method (semantic entropy) and is more expressive, able to distinguish uncertainty in cases where semantic entropy cannot
KLE works for both white-box and black-box LLMs and does not rely on token likelihoods
Provides concrete, effective design choices for practical implementation (e.g., graph kernels, weight functions)
Empirically demonstrates improved uncertainty quantification performance across multiple datasets and LLM architectures

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Word-Sequence Entropy (WSE): a method that calibrates uncertainty at both word and sequence levels, considering semantic relevance and generative inequality in LLM outputs","Bi-directional entailment-based semantic similarity: measuring semantic similarity between sequences to inform uncertainty allocation","Resampling based on uncertainty: filtering out high-uncertainty responses to improve model accuracy without additional fine-tuning or architectural changes"],"problems":["Lack of robust and general uncertainty estimation methods for free-form, open-ended medical question answering (QA) tasks","Bias in uncertainty quantification due to generative inequality (irrelevant words/sequences) in LLM-generated answers","Need for reliable uncertainty quantification to improve trustworthiness and safety in medical QA applications"],"datasets":["COVID-QA","Medical Meadow MedQA","PubMedQA","MedMCQA","MedQuAD"],"metrics":["AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)","Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"AUROC (PubMedQA, WSE vs SAR)","value":"WSE surpasses SAR by 4.99% AUROC"},{"metric":"Accuracy (COVID-QA, Zephyr-7B-Alpha, after WSE filtering)","value":"+6.36% improvement in model accuracy"}],"novelty_claims":["First proposal of Word-Sequence Entropy (WSE) for quantifying uncertainty in free-form, open-ended medical QA tasks, addressing generative inequality and semantic relevance.","WSE allocates uncertainty proportionally to relevant words and sequences, improving alignment with semantic reliability of LLM outputs.","Demonstrates that filtering responses based on WSE uncertainty scores can significantly improve LLM accuracy in medical QA without additional fine-tuning or architectural changes.","Extensive evaluation across five medical QA datasets and seven LLMs, showing WSE outperforms six baseline methods under two correctness criteria."]}

methods: Word-Sequence Entropy (WSE): a method that calibrates uncertainty at both word and sequence levels, considering semantic relevance and generative inequality in LLM outputs
Bi-directional entailment-based semantic similarity: measuring semantic similarity between sequences to inform uncertainty allocation
Resampling based on uncertainty: filtering out high-uncertainty responses to improve model accuracy without additional fine-tuning or architectural changes
problems: Lack of robust and general uncertainty estimation methods for free-form, open-ended medical question answering (QA) tasks
Bias in uncertainty quantification due to generative inequality (irrelevant words/sequences) in LLM-generated answers
Need for reliable uncertainty quantification to improve trustworthiness and safety in medical QA applications
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

results.value: WSE surpasses SAR by 4.99% AUROC
+6.36% improvement in model accuracy

novelty_claims: First proposal of Word-Sequence Entropy (WSE) for quantifying uncertainty in free-form, open-ended medical QA tasks, addressing generative inequality and semantic relevance.
WSE allocates uncertainty proportionally to relevant words and sequences, improving alignment with semantic reliability of LLM outputs.
Demonstrates that filtering responses based on WSE uncertainty scores can significantly improve LLM accuracy in medical QA without additional fine-tuning or architectural changes.
Extensive evaluation across five medical QA datasets and seven LLMs, showing WSE outperforms six baseline methods under two correctness criteria.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Shifting Attention to Relevance (SAR): an attention-shifting method that reassigns attention to more relevant tokens and sentences for uncertainty quantification in LLMs","Token-level and sentence-level relevance scoring by measuring semantic change when removing tokens/sentences","Reweighting uncertainty quantification based on relevance scores"],"problems":["Unreliable outputs and hallucinations in large language models (LLMs)","Inaccurate uncertainty quantification (UQ) in LLMs due to equal weighting of all tokens, regardless of their semantic relevance","Overvaluation of irrelevant tokens or sentences in UQ, leading to poor trustworthiness in LLM outputs, especially for free-form question answering"],"datasets":["CoQA","TriviaQA","SciQ","MedQA","MedMCQA"],"metrics":["Uncertainty quantification accuracy","Predictive entropy","Semantic entropy","Correlation between relevance and uncertainty proportion"],"results":[{"metric":"Uncertainty quantification performance (SAR vs. previous SOTA)","value":"SAR outperforms previous state-of-the-art by a large margin (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"},{"metric":"Token-level uncertainty (example)","value":"SAR: 0.650 (low uncertainty, correct answer returned); Entropy-based: 1.949 (high uncertainty, answer refused)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to systematically identify and address the impact of token- and sentence-level generative inequality on uncertainty quantification in LLMs","Proposes a simple yet effective method (SAR) that shifts attention to more relevant components (tokens/sentences) for improved UQ","Demonstrates that reweighting based on semantic relevance leads to superior UQ performance across multiple LLMs and domains"]}

methods: Shifting Attention to Relevance (SAR): an attention-shifting method that reassigns attention to more relevant tokens and sentences for uncertainty quantification in LLMs
Token-level and sentence-level relevance scoring by measuring semantic change when removing tokens/sentences
Reweighting uncertainty quantification based on relevance scores
problems: Unreliable outputs and hallucinations in large language models (LLMs)
Inaccurate uncertainty quantification (UQ) in LLMs due to equal weighting of all tokens, regardless of their semantic relevance
Overvaluation of irrelevant tokens or sentences in UQ, leading to poor trustworthiness in LLM outputs, especially for free-form question answering
metrics: Correlation between relevance and uncertainty proportion

results.metric: Uncertainty quantification performance (SAR vs. previous SOTA)
results.value: SAR outperforms previous state-of-the-art by a large margin (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)
SAR: 0.650 (low uncertainty, correct answer returned); Entropy-based: 1.949 (high uncertainty, answer refused)

novelty_claims: First to systematically identify and address the impact of token- and sentence-level generative inequality on uncertainty quantification in LLMs
Proposes a simple yet effective method (SAR) that shifts attention to more relevant components (tokens/sentences) for improved UQ
Demonstrates that reweighting based on semantic relevance leads to superior UQ performance across multiple LLMs and domains

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LM-Polygraph framework implementing state-of-the-art uncertainty estimation (UE) methods for LLMs in text generation","Combining multiple uncertainty scores as per recent research (e.g., Ren et al., 2023; Vazhentsev et al., 2023a)","Demo web application that displays confidence scores in chat dialogs","Extendable benchmark for consistent evaluation of UE techniques"],"problems":["Hallucination in large language models (LLMs), i.e., generating fabricated or misleading information","Lack of practical, engineering-focused uncertainty estimation (UE) tools for LLMs in text generation","Fragmentation and lack of comparability in existing UE research for LLMs"],"datasets":["BLOOMz","LLaMA-2","ChatGPT","GPT-4"],"metrics":["Uncertainty score (e.g., Mean Pointwise Mutual Information)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First comprehensive engineering framework (LM-Polygraph) implementing a wide range of state-of-the-art UE methods for LLMs in text generation tasks with unified Python interfaces.","Provides the ability to combine multiple uncertainty scores for improved estimation.","Introduces an extendable benchmark for consistent evaluation of UE techniques in text generation.","Offers a demo web application that integrates uncertainty scores into standard LLM chat dialogs for end-user and researcher benefit.","Supports a wide range of recent LLMs and is designed for future compatibility."]}

methods: LM-Polygraph framework implementing state-of-the-art uncertainty estimation (UE) methods for LLMs in text generation

metrics: Uncertainty score (e.g., Mean Pointwise Mutual Information)
novelty_claims: First comprehensive engineering framework (LM-Polygraph) implementing a wide range of state-of-the-art UE methods for LLMs in text generation tasks with unified Python interfaces.
Provides the ability to combine multiple uncertainty scores for improved estimation.
Introduces an extendable benchmark for consistent evaluation of UE techniques in text generation.
Offers a demo web application that integrates uncertainty scores into standard LLM chat dialogs for end-user and researcher benefit.
Supports a wide range of recent LLMs and is designed for future compatibility.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Stable Explanations Confidence: measuring LLM uncertainty by analyzing the distribution of generated explanations for an answer, treating each explanation as a test-time classifier and computing a posterior answer distribution via transductive marginalization.","Using explanation entailment as the classifier likelihood within the stable explanations confidence framework."],"problems":["Overconfidence of LLMs in incorrect responses, especially in high-risk applications.","Difficulty of adapting traditional uncertainty quantification methods to LLMs due to computational cost and closed-source models.","Limitations of existing black-box uncertainty quantification methods for LLMs, which often rely on heuristics and can still produce overconfident responses.","Need for well-calibrated confidence scores for LLM predictions, especially under distribution shift."],"datasets":["CommonsenseQA","TruthfulQA","MedQA","MMLU Professional Law","MMLU Conceptual Physics"],"metrics":["AURC (Area Under the Risk-Coverage Curve)","AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)"],"results":[{"metric":"AURC and AUROC (average across five datasets)","value":"Stable explanations confidence outperforms baselines, especially on more complex question-answering problems."}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new framework for LLM uncertainty quantification based on the stability of generated explanations, rather than self-verbalized confidence or response consistency.","Introduces the idea of treating each explanation as a test-time classifier and computing a posterior over answer distributions via transductive marginalization.","Demonstrates improved performance on selective uncertainty tasks (AURC, AUROC) compared to existing black-box methods across multiple datasets and LLMs."]}

methods: Stable Explanations Confidence: measuring LLM uncertainty by analyzing the distribution of generated explanations for an answer, treating each explanation as a test-time classifier and computing a posterior answer distribution via transductive marginalization.
Using explanation entailment as the classifier likelihood within the stable explanations confidence framework.
problems: Overconfidence of LLMs in incorrect responses, especially in high-risk applications.
Difficulty of adapting traditional uncertainty quantification methods to LLMs due to computational cost and closed-source models.
Limitations of existing black-box uncertainty quantification methods for LLMs, which often rely on heuristics and can still produce overconfident responses.
Need for well-calibrated confidence scores for LLM predictions, especially under distribution shift.
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

results.metric: AURC and AUROC (average across five datasets)
results.value: Stable explanations confidence outperforms baselines, especially on more complex question-answering problems.

novelty_claims: Proposes a new framework for LLM uncertainty quantification based on the stability of generated explanations, rather than self-verbalized confidence or response consistency.
Introduces the idea of treating each explanation as a test-time classifier and computing a posterior over answer distributions via transductive marginalization.
Demonstrates improved performance on selective uncertainty tasks (AURC, AUROC) compared to existing black-box methods across multiple datasets and LLMs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Shifting Attention to Relevance (SAR): an attention-shifting method that jointly examines the relevance of each token and sentence and reassigns attention for uncertainty estimation","Measuring token relevance by semantic change when removing a token from a sentence","Quantifying uncertainty proportions at token and sentence level"],"problems":["Uncertainty estimation of large language model (LLM) generations","Bias in uncertainty quantification due to generative inequalities (i.e., treating all tokens/sentences equally regardless of their semantic relevance)","Over-valuation of irrelevant tokens or sentences in uncertainty estimation"],"datasets":["CoQA","TriviaQA","SciQ","MedQA","MedMCQA"],"metrics":["Uncertainty estimation accuracy","Correlation between relevance and uncertainty proportion","Performance on free-form question-answering tasks"],"results":[{"metric":"Uncertainty estimation performance (SAR vs. previous SOTA)","value":"SAR outperforms previous state-of-the-art by a large margin (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)"},{"metric":"Token-level uncertainty (example)","value":"SAR Uncertainty = 0.650 (Low uncertainty, return generation) vs. Entropy-based Uncertainty = 1.949 (High uncertainty, refuse to answer)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to systematically investigate the effect of generative inequalities (token and sentence level) on uncertainty quantification in LLMs","Propose SAR, a method that shifts attention to more relevant tokens and sentences for improved uncertainty estimation","Demonstrate that SAR mitigates biases from generative inequalities and outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods on multiple LLMs and datasets"]}
